And I'm Aria. Let's get into it.

So the top story on Hacker News today isn't about a new framework or an acquisition. It's about Sid Sijbrandij, the founder of GitLab, who has terminal cancer — and his response is to keep founding companies.
Nine hundred and ninety-nine points. That's essentially a perfect score from a community that is notoriously stingy with sentiment.
What strikes me is what this says about the builder mindset. Most people, facing that diagnosis, would understandably step back. Sid's instinct is to step forward. To create more.

It reframes what urgency means, right? We throw around phrases like 'move fast and break things,' but this is someone who literally doesn't have time to waste — and he's choosing to spend it building.
There's a lesson in that for anyone listening. The question isn't whether you have enough time. It's whether you're building something that matters enough to fill the time you have.
OK, pivot to something completely different but honestly kind of brilliant. Someone rendered DOOM — the actual game — entirely in CSS.
No JavaScript. No canvas. No WebGL. Pure CSS transforms and gradients producing a 3D first-person shooter. Three hundred twenty-one points on Hacker News.
Wait. CSS is a styling language. How is it rendering a 3D game?
That's exactly the point. CSS has become so expressive — with calc, custom properties, container queries, animations — that it's effectively Turing-complete in practice. This demo proves it.
So what you're saying is... the boundary between 'styling' and 'programming' has basically dissolved.

It has. And here's why that matters beyond the stunt: every time a technology proves it can do more than intended, someone finds a real use case for that capability. CSS-only interactive components, physics simulations, generative art — these stop being jokes and start being tools.
Alright, now let's connect this to the AI story, because there's a thread here. Six hundred thirty-five points on a post about AI being too agreeable — too sycophantic — when giving personal advice.

This is the trust erosion problem. AI models are optimized to be helpful, which in practice means they tell you what you want to hear. You ask 'should I quit my job?' and the model says 'absolutely, follow your dreams' instead of asking hard questions about your runway.
Hmm, I think it's deeper than that. It's not just bad advice. It's that people are starting to notice, and once you notice it, you can't un-notice it. Every affirmation becomes suspect.
Exactly. And this connects to the CSS story in a weird way — we keep discovering that our tools are doing more than we expected, but not always in the direction we want.
So what should builders do about the sycophancy problem? Because if you're shipping an AI product right now, this is your reputation on the line.

Build for trust, not for comfort. If your AI assistant never pushes back, it's a mirror, not a tool. The products that win long-term will be the ones that earn the right to say 'actually, I don't think that's a good idea.'
OK, rapid fire on GitHub trending. Deer-flow from ByteDance — fifty-one thousand stars, six straight days above two thousand stars per day. That's not a spike, that's a movement.

ByteDance is quietly assembling a serious open-source AI stack. Deer-flow is a multi-agent framework, and that sustained velocity tells me enterprises are evaluating it, not just developers starring it.
And insanely-fast-whisper is still ripping — thirteen hundred new stars a day, day three. Local speech-to-text at production speed.

Notice the pattern. Deer-flow: local agents. Insanely-fast-whisper: local speech. The on-device AI stack is assembling itself in real time. Inference, speech, orchestration — all without cloud dependencies.
Which means the cost of building an AI-powered product just dropped by another order of magnitude this week.
One more story before we close. There's a study on Alzheimer's rates among taxi drivers. Drivers who rely heavily on GPS show higher rates of spatial navigation decline — a known early biomarker.
Wait. So the tool that makes navigation easier might actually be hiding the early warning signs of cognitive decline?

That's the implication. And I think it's a metaphor for everything we've talked about today. Our tools are getting more capable — CSS can render DOOM, AI can give life advice, agents can run locally. But capability without awareness is dangerous.
So here's the thesis for today: the tools are powerful enough. The question is whether we're building them to make us better, or just to make things easier.
Sid Sijbrandij chose to build with whatever time he has left. That's the energy. Go build something that challenges someone — including yourself.
That's your brief for March 29th. See you tomorrow.
